dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for an unconscious 88-year-old female at the given address.
I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What happened when you found your mother?
She was unconscious when I got home at 7:45 AM with snoring breathing and making strange sounds.
She's semi-conscious, unresponsive, but trying to look around. She reacts to loud noises.
Radial pulse is 119 and irregular, possibly irregularly so.
Breathing is 26 times a minute, slightly snoring.
Blood pressure is 180 over 116 mmHg.
Her oxygen saturation is 69%.
Her glucose is 114 mg/dL.
She doesn't appear to be in pain; pain level is 0.
Her eyes are open with a GCS of 4.
She responds with incomprehensible sounds, GCS 2.
Her motor response is to localize sound, GCS 3.
Her skin looks ashy with a buildup of dead skin cells.
Airway is patent and self-maintained.
She has a medical history of primary hypertension.
No known allergies.
Medications could not be established.
She has had a bowel incontinence episode.
Right eye shows cataract distortion, left pupil is sluggish.
Gonna place a 4-lead ECG as part of protocol for altered mental status.
Tried two 22-gauge IVs in her forearm, both unsuccessful.
Attempted a 12-lead ECG, but the rhythm was unclear at first.
Could not get a second blood pressure due to her self-movement.
She keeps hitting her own head; frequent intervention is needed to prevent that.
We did a limb lift to place her on the stretcher safely.
She's now on the stretcher with three straps. Rails are up.
Transporting her to [COMPANY_NAME] with lights and sirens due to the acuity.
Completed a verbal report to the receiving hospital prior to arrival.
ED is preparing her in room 15, we are close to arrival.
She reached out during transport, but movements were not purposeful.
She's loaded on the cot, and we're ready to transfer her to the hospital bed.
She requires constant redirection to prevent self-harm at all times.
Ambulance moves are smooth; patient is secured with three straps on the cot.
Skin feels warm but dry. No signs of cold or cyanosis on current evaluation.
Neck veins are not distended and unremarkable.
Responds poorly to verbal cues; hearing is questionable. No foreign bodies in the ears.
Looks clear and none of the airway compromise signs are present.
No focal tenderness on the chest or abdomen palpation.
Bowel sounds faint but present in all four quadrants.
Abdomen flat with no palpable masses or rigidity noted.
No focal neurological deficits found; external signs of stroke unobserved.
No facial or extremity asymmetry, though she appears to have left pupil sluggishness.
Her respiration remains at 26 shallow breaths per minute.
Peripheral pulses are strong, 119 still, although irregular in rhythm.
12-lead ECG was not read on the unit, but she has a sinus arrhythmia according to the hospital.
She does not show any brushing movement reactions in response to stimuli.
ED at [CITY] is being updated through our personal phone for arrival preparation.
Oxygen saturation remains at baseline 69% with multiple checks.
She is moving limbs but with no evidence of intention behind the movement.
All vital signs are being monitored en route; transport in progress with safe movement.
Estimating emergency transport time to [CITY] at about 10 minutes from initial pickup.
All pertinent information has been transferred directly to the receiving physician at the ED.
EMT report has been prepared for full ED handover with all narrative notes.
She still needs to be observed for spontaneous changes or deterioration in mental status.
Protocol 3-15 is followed through all the way for altered mental status care.
She does push at her face and head, but can't follow a command or grab.
She will be moved from the cot directly to the hospital bed by the Reeves stretcher method.
No transport incident to report; process smooth on loading and unloading.
Reaching the hospital now-arrival imminent.
Bed transfer with Reeves stretcher method is initiated in the receiving bay.
Nurse is present and present in the room. About to begin offloading procedure in earnest.
All her medications and past history are accounted for in documentation despite incomplete patient interview.
ED nurse confirms receipt of the patient and document sign-off in progress.
Call is officially closed with all steps fulfilled and complete transfer to ED achieved.
GCS remained stable at 11 throughout the transport; has not declined since scene arrival.
Updates will be provided via EMT portal once all offloading is complete.
All care notes and pro-procedure logs are suitably reviewed; ready for ED hand-off.
All equipment and cot have been removed from the ambulance safely.
No incidents occurred during transport, and strict adherence to patient care protocols was maintained.
Bilateral upper and lower extremities show no signs of neurological asymmetry.
EMT unit is now available again. Shift notes are cleaned up for next call update.
Final review done and EMT record submitted to the log. All steps taken in protocol fulfilled.
Patient now under hospital care with detailed report delivered. All EMS actions completed.
All equipment is secured, and the ambulance is cleared for next deployment.
Final notes are documented for quality improvement, incident will be reviewed for transport practices.
Equipment restocked in preparation for next emergency call. Vehicle clean and ready.
Full handover to ED has been given and signed for by the receiving staff.
All patient-derived vital signs and assessment from scene are accurately transmitted.
No additional devices or needs identified during handoff. ED assumes full responsibility.
Call officially wrapped up with no further action required by EMS after handing over to ED.
All EMT gear is unpacked and team is ready for dispatch back into service.
All data supporting this incident is securely filed and handled internally before closure.
Ready for next 911 call if any-team is rested and re-supplied post scene.
No other transportation facilities were needed, the ambulance made transport safely.
Incident has been well-documented and uploaded to EMR for future analysis.
Internal evaluation of this incident initiated for training, quality, and possible improvement.
This remains a closed incident, all parameters are documented, and the patient is now in proper care.
Final thoughts and review notes pending input and evaluation from team commander and EMT chief.
Team repeated PBIs and 30-minute rest to reset for any subsequent call.
All team members prepare handwritten notes for individual records on incident experiences.
Paperwork for this call is complete, and all forms used for filed in EMR.
Full team sign-out performed and incident file formally placed in files as of today.
Incident log is marked complete in all units for correct auditing and tracking purposes.
No more action required from us as of now; hospital assumes all care roles.
EMS fully exits the call; all medics clear the unit and wait for next call assignment.
Unit is ready for new calls and ready to exit station for further service.
